data.title: PLATTS: 100--US FERC report on enforcement actions shows settlements, refunds in FY2025
data.description: (Platts)--21Nov2025/323 pm EST/2023 GMT **24 new investigations opened, 17 closed in FY2025 **Pending cases include demand response penalties The US Federal Energy Regulatory Commission reported on market oversight actions in fiscal year 2025 that resulted in settlements with parties totaling $36.57 million. Several cases pending in courts or at the regulatory level are not included in that total. The number of settlements approved in FY2025 — 13 — is close to the 12 settlements approved in FY2024, and the report, released Nov. 20, shows similar data trends in many respects, FERC staff told commissioners during their Nov. 20 open meeting. The number of investigation cases opened and closed in the last two fiscal years is similar, as is the number of self-reports by companies on activities discovered that may trigger FERC enforcement action, FERC staff told Commissioner David LaCerte in response to questions. The money obtained through settlements includes $22.84 million in civil penalties and $13.73 million in disgorgement by the companies involved, according to the report (AD07-13). Those totals do not include a $5 million payment to resolve an administrative proceeding or a $30,000 penalty by a hydropower project owner, FERC said in the report. In August, FERC's Office of Enforcement was renamed the Office of Enforcement and Regulatory Accounting. The office is made up of three divisions, with one covering investigations, another on audits and accounting, and a third on analytics and surveillance. During FY2025, which ran from Oct 1, 2024, to Sept. 30, 2025, the Division of Audits and Accounting completed 10 audits of public utility, natural gas, and oil pipeline companies. The audits resulted in $80 million in refunds and other recoveries, 63 findings of noncompliance and 260 recommendations for corrective action, FERC said in the report. Among the cases that are pending in either court litigation or regulatory proceedings are proposed penalties of $722 million against American Efficient (IN24-2); $60 million against Rover Pipeline and Energy Transfer Partners (IN17-4, IN19-4); $25 million against Ketchup Caddy (IN23-14); and $15 million against Boyce Hydro Power (P-10809). FERC enforcement staff opened 24 new investigations in FY2025, while bringing 17 investigations to a close without further action, said Kevin Dinan, the staffer in the enforcement office who presented the report. "I sat in your seat," FERC Chairman Laura Swett told Dinan, when, about 10 years ago, she was in the enforcement office and presented the annual report to commissioners. "I know from personal experience that the industry waits with baited breath for this report every year," Swett said, because it shows FERC market oversight trends and enforcement activities. In the report, FERC included examples of referrals from regional grid market monitors that are reviewed and closed without an investigation. The companies or individuals are not identified, but "these summaries can be helpful to companies seeking to comply with the commission's regulations and orders," FERC said. When Swett asked Dinan about any trends seen in the current report, he said one constant in many of the cases is the need for companies to meet their obligations imposed by the terms of service they are providing. A power generator that signs up as a capacity resource, for example, needs to provide power at the level of timeliness and reliability upon which payment is made to avoid a violation, Dinan said. Similarly, for a company providing demand response services in an organized wholesale market, "it must have actual customers that agree to provide a response consistent, again, with the terms of the demand response program it agreed to participate in," Dinan told Swett. A good compliance department in any entity will be familiar with such commitments and work with its staff to ensure commitments are met, he said. That may have been a reference to a couple of the pending demand response penalty cases, in which Ketchup Caddy and American Efficient have been alleged to have been paid for demand response commitments that were not met, and with limited information on customers. SUPREME COURT CASE IMPLICATIONS In the American Efficient case, the company recently asked FERC to decline to issue the proposed penalty, repeating its claim that FERC's enforcement order violated Supreme Court precedent in SEC v. Jarkesy. In the FERC staff investigation and show cause order proposing a penalty, staff alleged that American Efficient bought sales data of energy-efficient products at retail outlets, calculated how much power would be saved with those products and bid the energy savings into capacity markets as if it caused the savings. FERC staff alleged that the company received millions of dollars in capacity payments from PJM Interconnection and the Midcontinent ISO without providing the promised benefits. The report states that aspects of FERC enforcement processes have been challenged in light of the Supreme Court's 2024 decision in SEC v. Jarkesy. In that case, the court said the US Securities and Exchange Commission's use of administrative law judges to adjudicate civil penalty actions in securities fraud cases violated the right to a jury trial under the Seventh Amendment of the Constitution. The report noted that the enforcement office's Division of Analytics and Surveillance, which examines energy trading and other activities, made improvements in its surveillance processes during FY2025 by using more sophisticated, interactive dashboards and moving analytic capabilities to cloud computing. The migration to cloud computing allowed access to nonpublic and commercial data feeds, enabling FERC staff to compile analyses that previously required ad-hoc activities, FERC said. — Thomas Tiernan, thomas.tiernan@spglobal.com-- Edited by Michael Lustig, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Wheat Futures Higher on Quiet Pre-Holiday Trading - Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Wheat for March delivery rose 0.3%, to $5.42 1/2 a bushel, on the Chicago Board of Trade on Friday, with trading seen as low volume ahead of next week's Thanksgiving holiday.
  • Soybeans for January delivery rose 0.2%, to $11.26 3/4 a bushel.
  • Corn for December delivery fell 0.1%, to $4.37 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Out of Office: Traders appeared to go into holiday mode, with wheat and the row crop complex in general showing only small moves. With the Thanksgiving holiday next week, traders seem to favor a risk-off approach. The holiday also marks the beginning of traders getting their books in order for the new year, which often involves liquidating long positions, said Charlie Sernatinger of Marex in a note, adding that if something moves the market next week then wheat may break out of its typical seasonal selling.

No Notice: The USDA didn't announce any fresh flash sales Friday of soybean exports to China, which concerned grain traders. "Stale length exits as China struggles with an abundance of domestic and world soybeans," said AgResource in a note. The most-active soybean contract is unchanged as analysts speculate how much China has purchased, and how much more it may buy. "China may add to its purchases on deeper corrections, but China has no willingness to chase a CBOT rally," said AgResource.

INSIGHT

Growth Potential: Demand for meat is likely to support growth in cattle and hog markets heading into the new year, said Teucrium Trading in a note. "Global income growth supports long-run protein consumption, sustaining baseline demand for feed grains," said Teucrium. Growing livestock supplies mean that more animal feed is needed, which favors soybean and corn futures.

Switching Things Up: Grain traders are concerned about demand for wheat globally, which in turn is pressuring prices in the short-term. But that is expected to turn the corner as 2026 progresses. "This is because the low price level--wheat prices on CBOT and Euronext fell to five-year lows in October--may have led to a reduction in the area sown with winter wheat," said Commerzbank in a note. The decrease in winter wheat planted around the world may make the supply situation tighter and raise prices. The firm moves its outlook for average 2026 wheat prices to $5.80 a bushel.

AHEAD

  • The USDA will release its weekly Grain Export Inspections report at 11 a.m. ET Monday.
  • The USDA and the CBOT will be closed in observance of Thanksgiving on Thursday.
  • The CBOT will be open for a half-day of trading, closing at 1:15 p.m. ET on Friday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: Kinetik Holdings Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
ICE Review: Weakness in Canola Ahead of Weekend
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Intercontinental Exchange canola futures fell back on Friday, as the recent rally turns into rangebound trading.

An analyst said that without any fresh news canola has "no appetite to go higher."

Today's losses saw the January canola contract slip behind its 20-day moving average, but it remained above the 50-day average.

Added to that, weakness in Chicago soyoil, MATIF rapeseed and Malaysian palm oil put pressure on canola. Increases in Chicago soybeans and soymeal tried to temper the declines. Losses in crude oil weighed on vegetable oil values.

There was some good news for canola, as the Canadian Grain Commission reported the oilseed's exports for the week ended Nov. 16 improved more than 57% at a marketing-year high of 284,600 tons. However, the year-to-date for 2025/26 of 1.83 million tons is slightly more than the year ago exports.

The Canadian dollar was a pinch lower on Friday afternoon with the loonie at 70.95 U.S. cents compared to Thursday's close of 71.02.

There were 38,768 contracts traded on Friday, compared to 39,056 on Thursday. Spreading accounted for 24,562 contracts traded.

 
Prices are in Canadian dollars per metric ton:

Jan     641.10    dn  9.10 
Mar     653.70    dn  9.30

data.title: PLATTS: 100--Alberta TIER carbon prices rally on federal budget support
data.description: (Platts)--21Nov2025/325 pm EST/2025 GMT **Prices rise 11.8% on week to reach C$19/mtCO2e Nov. 20 **Speculative demand, year-end positioning support prices Alberta's Technology Innovation and Emissions Reduction carbon prices have rebounded following the passage of Canada's federal budget on Nov. 17, which seeks to improve the effectiveness of Canada's carbon markets. Sources stated Nov. 20 that the federal budget's provisions for carbon markets serve as a key driver of renewed interest, providing greater policy certainty for emissions trading and strengthening Canada's carbon pricing framework. Platts assessed Alberta TIER Emission Performance Credits and Emission Offsets at C$19.00/metric ton of carbon dioxide equivalent on Nov. 20, up C$2/mtCO2e on the week. Platts is part of S&P Global Energy. Traded levels rose throughout the week, with a trade for EPCs and Offsets at C$19/mtCO2e on Nov. 20, the highest reported trade since the launch of the assessment on Aug. 1, 2025. "Sounds like the market expects new policy to support TIER and Canadian project development," one broker said. The broker previously noted that concerns were raised about the proposed elimination of the emissions cap on oil and gas in Canada, highlighting significant pushback unless there is strong support from markets like TIER. Canada is experiencing a political movement aimed at increasing exports, particularly of LNG and crude oil, the broker said, but added that environmental groups are advocating for higher carbon taxes and stricter emissions allowances to support this growth. Additionally, market participants have observed that the TIER market has reached the floor, prompting a turnaround in prices. A second broker explained that there was a lack of sellers when bid levels ranged from C$16.50/mtCO2e to C$17.60/mtCO2e, which required rising bid levels to attract supply. The second broker also noted a slight uptick in demand as the year-end approaches, with entities working to balance their emissions for 2025. Adding to the upward support, a trader heard that a US speculative entity has entered the Alberta carbon market, seeking profit opportunities that could further support prices. "I've heard that there are some new spec in the US looking to buy some credits, [which] should support prices a bit," the trader said. TIER prices have experienced an extended period of weakness due to persistent oversupply and downturn post proposed program updates on Sept. 16. However, market participants continue to express bullish sentiment while offering minimal feedback on where prices may settle. — Madeline Ryan, madeline.ryan@spglobal.com; David Lademan, david.lademan@spglobal.com-- Edited by Karina Roman, newsdesk@spglobal.com--Platts Electricity Alert--

data.title: Jacobs Solutions Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
Franklin Street Properties in Talks to Refinance Debt
data.description:
By Terell Wright

Franklin Street Properties is in active negotiations with a lender to refinance all of its current debt, commercial real-estate group said.

The company in May said its board of directors had initiated a review of ways to maximize shareholder value, including a potential sale or debt refinancing.

Shares rose 9.8%, to 98 cents, late Friday, though remain down 46% this year.

FSP Chief Executive George Carter said the company doesn't know of any specific reason for the recent decline in its shares.

Write to Terell Wright at terell.wright@wsj.com

data.title: BBC board member Banerji resigns after Trump documentary edit
data.description:
An independent director on the BBC's board, Shumeet Banerji, has quit on Friday in the wake of what the public broadcaster said was an incorrect edit of a speech by President Donald Trump and which has prompted the threat of a $5 billion lawsuit.

Banerji, who also serves on the board of Indian conglomerate Reliance Industries and is a former chief executive of management consultancy Booz & Company, resigned a few weeks before his four-year term was due to end, the broadcaster said.

The broadcaster's news arm reported that Banerji had said in a resignation letter that he was upset about governance issues at the top of the corporation.

In the letter, Banerji said he had not been consulted about events leading up to the resignations of the BBC's director general, Tim Davie, and the chief executive of BBC News, Deborah Turness, BBC News reported.

Davie and Turness quit on November 9 following accusations of bias at the broadcaster, including in the way it edited a speech by Trump that he delivered on January 6, 2021, before his supporters stormed the Capitol building in Washington.

The BBC apologised on November 13 for how its "Panorama" news programme had edited the footage of Trump's speech but said there was no legal basis for him to sue it for defamation.

The BBC is mostly funded by a mandatory 174.50 pound ($228.56) annual charge on British households that watch live television from any broadcaster or use the BBC's online video platform.

($1 = 0.7635 pounds)

data.title: Oil Futures Fall on U.S. Push for Russia-Ukraine Peace — Market Talk
data.description: Oil futures end the session and the week lower as the U.S. presses Ukraine to accept a plan to end the war with Russia. "Prior upside lead from the diesel market has shifted to the downside" on the peace plan, Ritterbusch says in a note. Talks could drag on as the plan appears heavily skewed in favor of Russia, but Ukraine is likely to hold off further attacks on Russian oil infrastructure while they proceed, "removing a significant amount of geopolitical risk premium from the market," the firm adds. WTI settles down 1.6% at $58.06 a barrel as January moves to the front of the curve. Brent falls 1.3% to $62.56. The benchmarks end the week down 3.4% and 3.2%, respectively. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: MATTHEWS INTERNATIONAL CORP SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Matthews International Corp, a diversified provider of brand solutions, memorialization products, and industrial technologies, has released its annual Form 10-K report for fiscal 2025. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

Matthews International Corp reported the following key financial metrics for fiscal 2025:

  • Sales: $1,497.7 million, reflecting a decrease from the previous year's $1,795.7 million primarily due to the divestiture of the SGK Business and lower sales in the Industrial Technologies and Memorialization segments.
  • Gross Profit: $507.6 million, down from $529.7 million in the prior year, impacted by the divestiture of the SGK Business, lower sales, and higher material and labor costs.
  • Operating Profit (Loss): $75.5 million, compared to an operating loss of $12.3 million in the previous year, benefiting from a gain on the sale of the SGK Business.
  • Net (Loss) Income: $(24.5) million, an improvement from a net loss of $(59.7) million in the prior year, influenced by the gain on the SGK Business sale and reduced selling and administrative expenses.
  • (Loss) Earnings Per Share: $(0.79), compared to $(1.93) in the previous year, reflecting the overall improvement in net income despite ongoing challenges.

Business Highlights

The company's business performance for fiscal 2025 included the following highlights:

  • Revenue Segments: Matthews International operates under three main segments: Memorialization, Industrial Technologies, and Brand Solutions. The Memorialization segment focuses on products for cemeteries, funeral homes, and crematories, including caskets and cremation-related products. Industrial Technologies includes energy storage solutions and warehouse automation technologies. Brand Solutions, post-divestiture, consists of the cylinders business and a 40% ownership interest in Propelis.
  • Geographical Performance: Approximately 71% of the company's sales were from North America, 24% from Europe, 3% from Asia, and 2% from other regions. The Memorialization segment's principal operations are in North America, Europe, and Australia, while the Industrial Technologies segment operates globally with subsidiaries in Sweden, Germany, and China.
  • Sales Units: The Memorialization segment experienced lower unit sales of caskets, bronze and granite memorial products, and cremation equipment, primarily due to a decline in U.S. death rates. The Industrial Technologies segment saw reduced sales in energy storage solutions and product identification systems.
  • New Product Launches: The Industrial Technologies segment has been developing dry electrode battery solutions, which are more cost-effective and environmentally sustainable than traditional wet electrode processes, potentially increasing demand in the electric vehicle market.
  • Future Outlook: The company is focusing on strategic alternatives to enhance shareholder value, including a comprehensive review of its business portfolio. The Memorialization segment is expected to be influenced by North American death rates and the trend towards cremation. The Industrial Technologies segment's growth is tied to economic conditions, new product development, and trends in electric vehicles and e-commerce.

Strategic Initiatives

Matthews International has undertaken several strategic initiatives to drive long-term growth and enhance shareholder value:

  • Strategic Review: The company has launched a comprehensive review of strategic alternatives for its entire portfolio, aiming to enhance shareholder value. This includes the recent divestiture of its interest in the SGK Business, contributing to the formation of Propelis, in which the company retains a 40% ownership.
  • Cost Reduction Actions: The company is planning further cost reduction actions for fiscal 2026, focusing on engineering and tooling operations in Europe and general administrative functions.
  • Capital Management: The company has been actively managing its capital through various initiatives. It repurchased $12.2 million of its own shares and paid dividends totaling $32.8 million. The company also reduced its long-term debt by $67.0 million and managed its liquidity through a $750 million senior secured revolving credit facility. Additionally, the company has a $300 million aggregate principal amount of 8.625% senior secured second lien notes due in 2027. The company also facilitated a voluntary supply chain finance program to optimize working capital.
  • Future Outlook: Looking ahead, the company plans to continue its strategic alternatives review to drive long-term value creation. It anticipates capital expenditures in the range of $30 million to $40 million for fiscal 2026, focusing on enhancing manufacturing efficiency and product quality. The company expects to generate sufficient cash from operations to fund these capital spending projects. Additionally, the company is exploring the sale of its Warehouse Automation business as part of its ongoing strategic review.

Challenges and Risks

Matthews International faces several challenges and risks that could impact its operations and financial performance:

  • Interest Rate Risk: The company is exposed to variable interest rates, which could increase borrowing costs and negatively affect profitability and liquidity.
  • Raw Material Price Fluctuations: Increased prices or shortages of raw materials could adversely affect operations and cash flows, especially if the company cannot pass these costs onto customers.
  • Trade Policy Changes: New or increased tariffs and changes in import/export regulations could materially affect operating results, particularly with the U.S. government's recent tariff announcements.
  • Goodwill Impairment: The company has a significant amount of goodwill and intangible assets, and any impairment could negatively impact financial results.
  • Cremation and Mortality Rates: Changes in these rates could affect the Memorialization segment's revenues.
  • Global Conflicts: Ongoing conflicts, such as the war in Ukraine, may impact operations, supply chains, and financial markets.
  • Acquisitions and Divestitures: The company faces risks related to integrating acquisitions and managing divestitures, which could affect expected returns and financial performance.
  • Intellectual Property Risks: The company is involved in litigation with Tesla over intellectual property rights, which could result in significant costs and operational disruptions.
  • Environmental Regulations: Compliance with environmental laws may lead to unforeseen expenses and liabilities.
  • Activist Shareholders: Actions by activist shareholders could disrupt operations and affect strategic decisions.

SEC Filing: MATTHEWS INTERNATIONAL CORP [ MATW ] - 10-K - Nov. 21, 2025

data.title: Fitch Rates Stonebriar ABF Issuer LLC's Senior Unsecured Debt 'BB/RR4'
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Chicago-21 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has assigned a 'BB' rating with a Recovery Rating of RR4 to the $750 million, 8.125% senior unsecured notes issued by Stonebriar ABF Issuer LLC (Stonebriar) (BB/Stable), due Dec. 15, 2030.

The assignment of the final ratings follows the receipt of documents conforming to the information previously received. Please see "Fitch Assigns Stonebriar ABF Issuer 'BB' IDR; Outlook Stable" at www.fitchratings.com.

Key Rating Drivers

Solid Franchise; Strong Asset Quality: The ratings reflect Stonebriar's niche position as a market leader in the equipment financing sector, strong asset quality, adequate unsecured funding mix, appropriate leverage, solid cash flow generation with consistent operating performance through various cycles, and the quality and experience of its senior management team.

Business Model and Concentrations Constrain Rating: Stonebriar's ratings are primarily constrained by the monoline nature and cyclicality of its business model, significant obligor concentration relative to other large equipment finance companies and the potential impact of new financial policies following the January 2025 organizational restructuring and management internalization within the parent company, Eldridge Industries (Eldridge).

Stable Outlook: The Stable Outlook reflects Fitch's expectation that Stonebriar's leverage and unsecured funding mix will remain within the 'bb' category benchmark ranges over the Outlook horizon and that the credit quality of the company's borrower base will remain sound.

RATING SENSITIVITIES Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--Leverage at or approaching 6.0x, on Fitch's basis;

--A material decline in profitability, such that pre-tax ROAA is sustained below 2%;

--Rapid asset growth that is not accompanied by a commensurate increase in the risk management infrastructure;

--Material deterioration in asset quality metrics; and/or

--Weakening of the liquidity profile.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Enhanced portfolio diversity;

--Sustained reduction in leverage, on Fitch's basis, below 4.5x;

--Further expansion of the firm's franchise and market position while maintaining strong asset quality and consistent earnings performance; and/or

--An increase in unsecured debt exceeding 35% of total debt.

DEBT AND OTHER INSTRUMENT RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

DEBT AND OTHER INSTRUMENT RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

The senior unsecured debt is equalized with Stonebriar's Long-Term IDR, reflecting the funding mix and average recovery prospects for unsecured debtholders in a stress scenario due to the size of the unencumbered asset base.

DEBT AND OTHER INSTRUMENT RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

The senior unsecured debt rating is primarily sensitive to changes in Stonebriar's Long-Term IDR and, secondarily, to the funding mix and level of unencumbered balance sheet assets in a stress scenario relative to the outstanding debt. A decline in unencumbered asset coverage, combined with a material increase in secured debt, could result in notching the unsecured debt down from the Long-Term IDR.

ADJUSTMENTS

The Standalone Credit Profile (SCP) has been assigned in line with the implied SCP.

The Business Profile score has been assigned below the implied score due to the following adjustment reason(s): Business model (negative).

The Asset Quality score has been assigned below the implied score due to the following adjustment reason(s): Concentrations; asset performance (negative).

The Earnings & Profitability score has been assigned below the implied score due to the following adjustment reason(s): Revenue diversification (negative).

Date of Relevant Committee 05-Nov-2025 Sources of Information

The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. ESG Considerations

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visitwww.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

Stonebriar ABF Issuer LLC

----senior unsecured; Long Term Rating; New Rating; BB

data.title: Ross Stores Is Maintained at Buy by Citigroup
Massachusetts Plans $1.02 Billion Sale of GO and GO Refunding Bonds
data.description:
By Josh Beckerman

Massachusetts plans to sell $1.02 billion of bonds including $750 million of Series G general obligation bonds and $268.6 million of Series B general obligation refunding bonds.

The retail order period for the tax-exempt bonds is expected to be Dec. 2, with institutional pricing planned for Dec. 3 and closing anticipated Dec. 11, according to a roadshow document posted on MuniOS.

The new money bonds will have maturities of 2028 through 2035 and 2039 through 2055. The largest component, a $220 million term bond, is expected to be due in 2055.

The refunding bonds are expected to mature from 2027 through 2036 and 2039 through 2046.

Massachusetts discusses credit highlights in the roadshow document including a broad and diverse economy, a Budget Stabilization Fund estimated at $8.2 billion at the end of fiscal 2025 and the nation's highest 2024 per-capita income of $93,927.

The bond cap under the 2026-2030 capital investment plan is $3.23 billion. The largest component is $1.08 billion for MassDOT.

The bonds have ratings of Aa1 from Moody's and AA+ from S&P Global Ratings and Fitch ratings.

BofA Securities is the lead underwriter.

Write to Josh Beckerman at josh.beckerman@wsj.com

data.title: Fitch Affirms New American Funding's U.S. RMBS Servicer Ratings
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-New York-21 November 2025:

Fitch Ratings has affirmed the following servicer ratings for New American Funding, LLC (NAF):

--U.S. primary prime servicer rating at 'RPS3+'.

The Rating Outlook is Positive.

New American Funding

------RMBS Primary Prime Servicer; RMBS Primary Servicer - Prime Rating; Affirmed; RPS3+; Rating Outlook Positive

Key Rating Drivers

NAFs' 'RPS3+'' rating and Positive Outlook reflect its experienced management team and staff, developed corporate growth strategy, efficient technology environment, and effective risk management.

NAF has a tenured management team with more than 10 years of experience servicing residential mortgage loans. NAF employs 358 Full-Time Equivalents (FTEs) dedicated to the loan servicing division, including an owned affiliate in India (NAF India) with 40 FTEs. The company has retained a seasoned management group with more than 30 years of industry experience and nine years of company tenure.

Group managers average 20 years of loan servicing experience and more than eight years of company tenure. Company turnover, for the 12-month period ending June 30, 2025, was strong in Fitch's view compared other Fitch rated servicers at 11.60% voluntary and 8.20% involuntary turnover.

NAF has a developed corporate growth strategy. During the review period, NAF hired an executive to lead the retail sales channel to propel origination and servicing growth. NAF's Senior Management Team, in response to aggressive secondary pricing for mortgage servicing rights, launched a correspondent channel to sell production servicing released at opportune times.

NAF continues to focus on originating and servicing Government and Agency loans, and experienced servicing growth, as measured by unpaid principal balance (UPB) of 3.8% over the last 12 months. NAF currently services 278,048 loans totalling $72.3 billion of UPB.

Technology innovation is central to NAF's strategy. NAF uses the Mortgage Servicing Platform (MSP), an ICE Mortgage Technology loan servicing system, as their internal servicing system. Fitch views MSP as a preferred system in the servicing industry. NAF has continued to enhance their loan and default administration functionality through enhancements to MSP and maintains the ability to grow their portfolio as a result of adopting this technology.

NAF maintains a robust risk management environment and employs a multichannel compliance management system that includes quality control (QC), internal audit (IA) and vendor oversight, as well as compliance and litigation management. QC is outsourced to a third-party vendor and managed internally. Monthly QC is performed across 19 servicing business processes, each with an internally approved target defect rate. QC results are reviewed with the Senior Management Team every month to identify trends and opportunities for improvement.

IA is performed internally and conducts testing on 14 auditable servicing processes. The frequency of the audits is based on internal risk assessments conducted by the Compliance department. Processes identified as high-risk, or those exhibiting elevated error rates or control weaknesses, are prioritized for review in the following audit cycle to ensure timely attention and mitigation of potential risks.

Fitch does not publicly rate NAF's credit and financial strength. However, Fitch's financial institutions group reviewed its financial statements to provide an internal assessment, as a company's financial condition is a component of Fitch's servicer rating analysis.

In 2023, NAF converted from a California S corporation to a Delaware LLC. It is an independent mortgage lender based in Orange County, CA that was founded as a loan processing and underwriting consumer call center in 2003. The company's loan servicing business is headquartered in Austin, TX, with functions such as document custodial management and loan boarding located in Tustin, CA.

The company is a Fannie Mae, Freddie Mac and Ginnie Mae direct lender, seller and servicer that operates its own builder and real estate-based lending divisions. Management represents that about 70% of new loan origination business is sourced through its retail channel while about 30% is sourced via its call center operation. The company continues to pursue nonqualified mortgage (NQM) lending, call center loan production and subservicing to build volume and diversify revenue.

Historically, the company originated loans that were sold or subserviced by a third party. In 2014, management decided to bring all servicing in-house and assembled a servicing team to build the necessary systems and processes. In 2014, the initial group of NAF-originated loans was onboarded into the servicing platform. In 2015, NAF transferred all loans subserviced by a third party in-house.

As of June. 30, 2025, NAF's primary servicing portfolio comprised 278,048 loans totalling approximately $72.3 billion. This includes 272,410 GSE and government loans totalling $69.4 billion, 5,638 owned and third party serviced loans totalling $2.8 billion. Included in these categories are 548 closed-end second-lien loans totalling $47.7 million.

RATING SENSITIVITIES

Fitch rates residential mortgage primary, master and special servicers on a scale of 1 to 5, with 1 being the highest rating. Within some of these rating levels, Fitch further differentiates ratings by plus (+) and minus (-) as well as the flat rating. For more information on Fitch's residential servicer rating program, please see Fitch's report "Criteria for Rating U.S. Residential and Small Balance Commercial Mortgage Service," dated February 2020 and available at www.fitchratings.com.

DETAILS OF PERMISSIBLE SERVICE(S) FOR WHICH THE LEAD ANALYST IS BASED IN AN ESMA- OR FCA-REGISTERED FITCH RATINGS COMPANY (OR BRANCH OF SUCH A COMPANY) OR ANCILLARY SERVICE(S) CAN BE FOUND ON THE ENTITY SUMMARY PAGE FOR THIS ISSUER ON THE FITCH RATINGS WEBSITE.

In issuing and maintaining its ratings and in making other reports (including forecast information), Fitch relies on factual information it receives from issuers and underwriters and from other sources Fitch believes to be credible. Fitch conducts a reasonable investigation of the factual information relied upon by it in accordance with its ratings methodology, and obtains reasonable verification of that information from independent sources, to the extent such sources are available for a given security or in a given jurisdiction. The manner of Fitch’s factual investigation and the scope of the third-party verification it obtains will vary depending on the nature of the rated security and its issuer, the requirements and practices in the jurisdiction in which the rated security is offered and sold and/or the issuer is located, the availability and nature of relevant public information, access to the management of the issuer and its advisers, the availability of pre-existing third-party verifications such as audit reports, agreed-upon procedures letters, appraisals, actuarial reports, engineering reports, legal opinions and other reports provided by third parties, the availability of independent and competent third- party verification sources with respect to the particular security or in the particular jurisdiction of the issuer, and a variety of other factors. Users of Fitch’s ratings and reports should understand that neither an enhanced factual investigation nor any third-party verification can ensure that all of the information Fitch relies on in connection with a rating or a report will be accurate and complete. Ultimately, the issuer and its advisers are responsible for the accuracy of the information they provide to Fitch and to the market in offering documents and other reports. In issuing its ratings and its reports, Fitch must rely on the work of experts, including independent auditors with respect to financial statements and attorneys with respect to legal and tax matters. Further, ratings and forecasts of financial and other information are inherently forward-looking and embody assumptions and predictions about future events that by their nature cannot be verified as facts. As a result, despite any verification of current facts, ratings and forecasts can be affected by future events or conditions that were not anticipated at the time a rating or forecast was issued or affirmed.

The information in this report is provided “as is” without any representation or warranty of any kind, and Fitch does not represent or warrant that the report or any of its contents will meet any of the requirements of a recipient of the report. A Fitch rating is an opinion as to the creditworthiness of a security. This opinion and reports made by Fitch are based on established criteria and methodologies that Fitch is continuously evaluating and updating. Therefore, ratings and reports are the collective work product of Fitch and no individual, or group of individuals, is solely responsible for a rating or a report. The rating does not address the risk of loss due to risks other than credit risk, unless such risk is specifically mentioned. Fitch is not engaged in the offer or sale of any security. All Fitch reports have shared authorship. Individuals identified in a Fitch report were involved in, but are not solely responsible for, the opinions stated therein. The individuals are named for contact purposes only. A report providing a Fitch rating is neither a prospectus nor a substitute for the information assembled, verified and presented to investors by the issuer and its agents in connection with the sale of the securities. Ratings may be changed or withdrawn at any time for any reason in the sole discretion of Fitch. Fitch does not provide investment advice of any sort. Ratings are not a recommendation to buy, sell, or hold any security. Ratings do not comment on the adequacy of market price, the suitability of any security for a particular investor, or the tax-exempt nature or taxability of payments made in respect to any security. Fitch receives fees from issuers, insurers, guarantors, other obligors, and underwriters for rating securities. Such fees generally vary from US$1,000 to US$750,000 (or the applicable currency equivalent) per issue. In certain cases, Fitch will rate all or a number of issues issued by a particular issuer, or insured or guaranteed by a particular insurer or guarantor, for a single annual fee. Such fees are expected to vary from US$10,000 to US$1,500,000 (or the applicable currency equivalent). The assignment, publication, or dissemination of a rating by Fitch shall not constitute a consent by Fitch to use its name as an expert in connection with any registration statement filed under the United States securities laws, the Financial Services and Markets Act of 2000 of the United Kingdom, or the securities laws of any particular jurisdiction. Due to the relative efficiency of electronic publishing and distribution, Fitch research may be available to electronic subscribers up to three days earlier than to print subscribers.

Fitch Ratings, Inc. is registered with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission as a Nationally Recognized Statistical Rating Organization (the “NRSRO”). While certain of the NRSRO’s credit rating subsidiaries are listed on Item 3 of Form NRSRO and as such are authorized to issue credit ratings on behalf of the NRSRO (see https://www.fitchratings.com/site/regulatory), other credit rating subsidiaries are not listed on Form NRSRO (the “non-NRSROs”) and therefore credit ratings issued by those subsidiaries are not issued on behalf of the NRSRO. data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Gains As Traders Focus on Weather — Market Talk
data.description: U.S. natural gas futures rise ahead of the weekend as the market keeps close watch on shifts in the weather outlook, particularly for signs of further cold in December. Changing weather forecasts have kept natural gas prices volatile, with traders typically seeing more risk heading into the weekend. "I think the bears will be more nervous than the bulls about what that Sunday map will look like," Scott Shelton of TP ICAP says in a note. Nymex natural gas for December delivery settles up 2.4% at $4.58/mmBtu, up 0.3% on the week. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)
